perturbation.original_text: (b) Restrictions. Each party agrees that, during the Term and for two (2) years thereafter: (i) it will not disclose to any third party any Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other party except as expressly permitted in this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Restrictions. Each party agrees that, during the Term and for one (1) year thereafter: (i) it will not disclose to any third party any Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other party except as expressly permitted in this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from two years to one year may conflict with trade secret laws, which protect confidential business information for as long as it remains a trade secret and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York trade secret law protects confidential information indefinitely as long as it remains a trade secret. Reducing the protection period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, thereby undermining the protections afforded under New York law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a two-year confidentiality period, whereas the changed contract reduces this to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this reduction could conflict with trade secret laws, which protect confidential information indefinitely, as long as it remains a trade secret. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly address trade secret protection, the explanations provided establish that reducing the period undermines established protections, creating a legal contradiction because it potentially fails to provide adequate protection.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon written notice if the other party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after the date that it receives written notice of such breach from the non-breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon written notice if the other party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within ten (10) days after the date that it receives written notice of such breach from the non-breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days may be considered unreasonable and could conflict with general principles of contract law regarding adequate opportunity to cure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Opportunity to Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 2-508 implies a reasonable time for cure. Reducing the cure period to 10 days might not be deemed 'reasonable' in many commercial contexts, especially for complex breaches, potentially conflicting with common law principles as reflected in the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-508. Cure by Seller of Improper Tender or Delivery; Replacement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-508. Cure by Seller of Improper Tender or Delivery; Replacement. (1) Where any tender or delivery by the seller is rejected because non-conforming and the time for performance has not yet expired, the seller may seasonably notify the buyer of his intention to cure and may then within the contract time make a conforming delivery. (2) Where the buyer rejects a non-conforming tender which the seller had reasonable grounds to believe would be acceptable with or without money allowance the seller may if he seasonably notifies the buyer have a further reasonable time to substitute a conforming tender. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days, which may be deemed unreasonable. UCC § 2-508, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies a 'reasonable time' for cure. While the snippet focuses on the seller's right to cure, the principle of providing a reasonable time to remedy a breach is a general concept in contract law. Reducing the cure period to 10 days could be seen as conflicting with this principle, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially deprives the breaching party of a fair opportunity to rectify the breach, especially for complex situations.

perturbation.original_text: Within fifteen (15) days after the Effective Date or on December 31, 2015 (whichever is earlier), Licensor shall (at Licensor's sole expense), make the Titles available either online or by hard drive to Licensee or the third-party vendor specified by Licensee to provide or deliver the Titles from Licensee's or its third-party vendor's facilities.
perturbation.changed_text: Within thirty (30) days after the Effective Date or on December 31, 2015 (whichever is earlier), Licensor shall (at Licensor's sole expense), make the Titles available either online or by hard drive to Licensee or the third-party vendor specified by Licensee to provide or deliver the Titles from Licensee's or its third-party vendor's facilities. If Licensor fails to deliver any of the titles, no penalty will be incurred.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that 'no penalty will be incurred,' Licensee is essentially removing their ability to claim breach of contract in the event of failure to deliver the titles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344, a party is entitled to damages for breach of contract. By saying that no penalty will be incurred, the licensee eliminates one of their remedies, which is the legal right to seek damages for breach of contract.

perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly states that 'no penalty will be incurred,' effectively removing the licensee's ability to claim breach of contract in the event of failure to deliver the titles. According to the Law Explanation, this directly contradicts the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344, which entitles a party to damages for breach of contract. The changed text removes the legal right to seek damages, creating a legal contradiction under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, where a contractual term violates federal or state law, making it unenforceable.
